event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a space-themed animation and a narrator discussing the International Space Station, transitioning to a scale comparison between the Giant Spherical Structure (Las Vegas) and the ISS, with Deanan Dasilva explaining the immersive visual experience possible with the MS6 SPHERE camera rig for scientific exploration. Ziad Sultan of Spotify then appears as upbeat electronic music starts, leading to a smartphone displaying Spotify and a 'Listening for music' screen. The narrator reveals Shazam's high volume of daily identifications and explains its audio fingerprinting process using spectrogram and grid visualizations, concluding with 'THE TECH BEHIND SHAZAM' title card and a Nokia 3310 symbolizing technological evolution. The video then details 'AUDIO FINGERPRINTING' with a spectrogram, showing Shazam's initial challenge of digitizing music due to a lack of databases, as Chris Barton describes hiring staff to manually input data from 100,000 CDs. Early Shazam team photos and a spinning CD with binary code further illustrate the transition to digital, followed by a man pointing at a projected spectrogram and the narrator explaining how spectrograms map frequency and loudness over time. Julius Smith, an early Shazam consultant, explains spectrograms with a whistling noise example and discusses music's complex spectrograms due to harmonics, demonstrating the 'ba-ba-ba-BAM' motif from Beethoven's Fifth Symphony on both sheet music and a spectrogram. The narrator explains Shazam simplifies spectrograms by focusing on 'peaks,' transforming them into scatter plots, and Julius Smith illustrates how the brain handles 'superpositions of sounds' amidst background noise, leading to a dynamic grid visualization labeled 'SONG 8'. The narrative shifts to a coffee shop scene where Shazam identifies 'Minuet Recomposed' by Edward McCormack, referencing Bach's Minuet in G major, and shows a photo of the Shazam team, highlighting its 2002 launch before smartphones. Archival footage depicts early mobile phone use, with Chris Barton describing the limited functionality of phones like the Nokia 3310, and an early pitch deck for Shazam's dial-up service is introduced. The video then shows a Nokia 3310 with a Shazam notification for T.Rex's 'Jeepster,' and Chris Barton explains how Shazam's algorithm was improved to handle tempo and pitch adjustments in live music environments, illustrated by concert and DJ scenes. Despite early struggles and patent sales, Shazam eventually became profitable in 2016, with Barton reflecting on the company's resilience. Chris Barton explains Shazam's user adoption surge post-2008 App Store launch, followed by the narrator announcing Apple's 2018 acquisition, with a WSJ headline confirming the deal. Barton discusses music discovery in the streaming era, and the narrator highlights Shazam's integration with Apple Music's catalog. Barton demonstrates the app identifying songs by Bad Bunny and The Black Keys, reflecting on the importance of timing for startups, and the segment concludes with the Shazam icon in Apple's Control Center and credit screens.

He is heard speaking about the massive screen's ability to create a realistic spacewalk experience, and later, another male voice (likely also him or a colleague from Sphere) discusses using the venue and technology for science and future exploration.

He speaks with a clear American accent, explaining how a spectrogram of music changes with the introduction of noise, such as people walking into a bar and making sounds, and highlights the brain's ability to understand 'superpositions of sounds,' suggesting computers can replicate this.

This visual is accompanied by a male speaker's voice describing the limited functions of early mobile phones, such as making calls, sending texts, and playing Snake, setting the context for their usage.

It is shown with a large white Apple logo overlaid, symbolizing Apple’s acquisition, and its functionality is demonstrated by Chris Barton and described by a female narrator as one of the most popular free music apps, capable of identifying over 100 million songs from the Apple Music catalog.

Wall Street Journal Headline.description: A title card from The Wall Street Journal reading 'Apple Acquires Shazam and Its Song-Recognition App,' dated December 11, 2017, with the byline 'Tripp Mickle,' displayed over a blurred office scene.

Control Center.description: Apple’s Control Center interface shown on a smartphone screen, featuring the Shazam icon among other controls like Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and brightness, indicating app integration.

Credit Screens.description: Black screens listing credits for footage and animation, including names like Karl Mollohan, Corey Eisenstein, Sean Harvey, Ryan Trefes, Michael Tabb, Luca Depardon, and sources such as Google, Getty Images, and Shazam, accompanied by light, upbeat instrumental background music.

Apple Logo.description: A large white Apple logo overlaid on the smartphone screen displaying the Shazam app, symbolizing Apple’s acquisition of Shazam.

raw_segments: The video opens with a dramatic space-themed animation showing Earth and the Moon from a spacecraft’s perspective, as a female narrator discusses making things "easier to use" and the "next big step" of getting something onto the International Space Station. This transitions into a comparison graphic emphasizing the massive scale of the Giant Spherical Structure (Las Vegas) at 366 FT next to the International Space Station at 356 FT. A male voice, likely Deanan Dasilva, then explains that "the screen is so big that the ISS can actually fit inside the screen," allowing for a realistic "spacewalk" experience, as the visual shows him, identified as Principal at Big Sky Technology, Sphere Entertainment, adjusting a large spherical camera rig labeled 'MS6 SPHERE' in a workshop. Another male voice adds that "one of the things that we're very interested in as Sphere is to use the venue and the technology for science," continuing to describe "the type of things that we're sort of curious about and will potentially explore in the future." The scene shifts to Ziad Sultan, Vice President of Personalization at Spotify, speaking in an office with colorful cubed wall panels, as upbeat electronic background music begins. The video transitions to a close-up of a smartphone displaying Spotify’s interface, where a user selects a song, triggering a 'Listening for music' screen. The female narrator returns, revealing that "more than 23,000 songs are Shazamed every minute in the world," posing the question of "how does it identify the music around you after recording just seconds of audio from your phone?" Abstract data visualizations—first a spectrogram with orange dots, then a grid of blue dots with connecting lines—illustrate this process as the narrator explains that "Shazam has to create a unique audio fingerprint of what you're hearing, and then search its database for a matching fingerprint," which means "simplifying sound to a bunch of data that can be processed incredibly fast." The title card 'THE TECH BEHIND SHAZAM' appears over a vibrant digital background, with the narrator concluding, "This is the tech behind Shazam." The segment concludes with a vintage Nokia 3310 phone centered on a blue concentric wave background, symbolizing the evolution of music recognition technology, as the female narrator begins to explain that "the idea behind Shazam is simple: Take the audio captured on your..." before the audio cuts off, with the upbeat electronic music still playing.

The minute opens with a retro-style screen displaying a grid of yellow dots, which transitions to a spectrogram overlaid with the text 'AUDIO FINGERPRINTING'. A female narrator explains the process of converting phone audio into a unique 'audio fingerprint' that computers can identify, even amidst background noise. The scene then shifts to a retail store where a person browses through CDs, as the narrator continues, detailing how Shazam's initial step involved digitizing a vast amount of music due to the absence of an existing database. Chris Barton, co-founder of Shazam, appears in an office setting with a cityscape visible behind him, speaking directly to the camera. He articulately describes how they hired '30 18-year-olds' to work 24 hours a day in shifts, manually putting CDs into computers running custom software. He explains that they built a database of '100,000 CDs,' equating to about '1.7 million songs,' by typing in every song, album title, and artist name because CDs lacked metadata. A photo of four men, including Barton, is shown, representing the early Shazam founding team. The video returns to Barton speaking, then cuts back to the CD store, emphasizing the transition from physical to digital music. A spinning CD against a dark background with binary code further symbolizes digital data. A detailed spectrogram with frequency and time axes is displayed, followed by a man in a dark shirt pointing at a large projected spectrogram in a dimly lit room, illustrating audio analysis. The female narrator returns, explaining that after digitization, songs were converted into spectrograms, which map frequency content over time and display signal power levels (loudness) represented by color. The minute concludes with a different male voice beginning to describe a 'spectrogram of a bird.' The WSJ logo remains consistently in the top right corner throughout.

The video opens with Julius Smith, Emeritus Professor at Stanford University, standing in a dimly lit studio, gesturing toward a large projection screen displaying a vibrant spectrogram with purple and orange frequency patterns. He explains that time progresses to the right and frequency is on the vertical axis, demonstrating a sound with a distinct whistling noise. A female narrator then introduces Julius Smith as an early consultant for Shazam, as the scene transitions to a close-up photo of Chris Barton, a Shazam co-founder, smiling in front of a guitar. The narrator continues, explaining that spectrograms for music are complicated due to harmonics, while a full-screen spectrogram with a grid overlay dynamically shows frequency changes. The screen then splits, displaying the spectrogram on the left and classical music notation for Beethoven's Fifth Symphony on the right. Julius Smith gestures across both, vocalizing the iconic "ba-ba-ba-BAM" motif and explaining how it appears in both the score and the spectrogram, which is then accompanied by a dramatic orchestral rendition of the motif. The camera pans back to show Julius Smith continuing his explanation in the studio, with the WSJ logo consistently visible in the top right corner. The narrator begins to speak about Shazam matching spectrograms before the segment ends.

The video opens with a split-screen view, displaying a vibrant spectrogram with purple and orange frequency patterns scrolling horizontally on the left, while classical sheet music titled 'V. SYMPHONIE' with 'Allegro con brio' and 'Secondo' is shown on the right. A female narrator explains that Shazam simplifies spectrograms by focusing on 'peaks,' which are the primary elements the brain processes, transforming the complex visual into a scatter plot of peaks, even as background noise might introduce additional, meaningless ones. The scene transitions to a full-screen spectrogram, with the WSJ logo appearing consistently in the top right corner. This spectrogram then evolves into a dynamic grid of yellow, red, and pink dots, visualizing audio or musical elements. Julius Smith, an Emeritus Professor at Stanford University, appears in a dimly lit studio, wearing a dark cap and a green button-up shirt, gesturing expressively toward a large projection screen displaying the spectrogram and sheet music. Speaking with a clear American accent, he illustrates how a spectrogram of music changes as 'noise gets brought in,' such as people walking into a bar, drinking, and making sounds, resulting in the original spectrogram plus new activity. He emphasizes that the brain is very good at understanding 'superpositions of sounds,' and if humans can do it, computers can too. The video returns to the scrolling spectrogram, which continues to shift in intensity, before transitioning back to the grid of dots, now with blue and orange patterns, indicating a dynamic data representation. The grid then zooms out to reveal a larger, more complex pattern, with the text 'SONG 8' appearing at the bottom. The narrator concludes by explaining that Shazam's core technology involves matching these scatter plots, which essentially chart the most powerful signals at different frequencies over time, using important processing steps to quickly search its database for a match of these points in the proper sequence.

The segment opens with a dynamic grid visualization labeled 'SONG 8' from the WSJ, featuring scattered yellow, red, and blue dots on a dark blue grid, as a female narrator explains how a process handles multiple points at once without worrying about the overall sequence until the end. She then sets a scene, saying, 'Say you were in a coffee shop playing classical music,' as the video transitions to a coffee shop interior with a barista in a red apron. A close-up of a hand holding a smartphone appears, followed by a spectrogram with orange dots overlaid, which the narrator identifies as the first step: 'First, it makes a spectrogram like this.' The grid visualization reappears, showing yellow, then blue, and finally pink dots connected by lines, illustrating the narrator's description of isolating peaks and making a scatter plot, then connecting nearby peaks to form pairs. A smartphone screen displays the Shazam app searching for 'Minuet Recomposed' by Edward McCormack, while the narrator details how Shazam searches for matching pairs in a database of millions of songs to 'name the tune,' specifically mentioning 'Bach's Minuet in G major.' A group photo of the Shazam team, credited to Chris Barton, with a 'SHAZAM!' logo behind them, is shown as the narrator explains Shazam launched in 2002, 'well before the smartphone era.' Archival footage then shows people using early mobile phones, including a man in a pink shirt with a purple flip phone and a woman dancing with a phone, displaying a speech bubble 'I love that song!' and a Shazam tag for T.Rex's 'Jeepster.' A male speaker, likely Chris Barton, describes the limited functionality of these early mobile phones, stating, 'Everyone was carrying these mobile phones around everywhere and really you could only just do two things with them. You could make phone calls and you could send text messages. And I guess you could also play the game Snake.' The narrator then introduces an early pitch deck for Shazam, which was initially a service in the UK accessed by dialing. The segment concludes with a classic Nokia 3310 phone centered on a blue concentric wave background, displaying 'Menu' and the time '16:57,' symbolizing early mobile technology.

The video opens with a close-up of a classic Nokia 3310 phone, its green screen displaying a Shazam notification for T.Rex’s 'Jeepster' against a blue concentric wave background, as a female narrator explains that early Shazam allowed users to record 15 seconds of audio, disconnect, and receive a text with the song's name. The scene transitions to Chris Barton, co-founder of Shazam, speaking in a modern office with a cityscape visible behind him. The narrator continues, noting that while the core algorithm remained, engineers improved recognition. Barton, in his own voice, then explains the technology was "good enough that it felt like it worked most of the time and it really delighted people that it would work in these like bars and clubs and so on." He continues, detailing the need for "additional tweaks to deal with the fact that music in the real world also gets adjusted in terms of speed, tempo and pitch," citing how "DJs might adjust the speed of music when they're playing vinyl records." As he speaks, the narrative shifts to a vibrant concert scene with a band performing under colorful lights, audience members recording with phones, followed by close-ups of a DJ’s hands manipulating knobs and turntables under purple and blue lighting, visually illustrating the live music environment and challenges. A large illuminated Shazam logo is seen on an office wall, and a smartphone displays the modern Shazam app interface, as the narrator reveals Shazam wasn't an immediate hit, selling patents to fund development and not becoming profitable until 2016. A still photo of Chris Barton, credited 'COURTESY CHRIS BARTON', shows him with headphones, as the narrator mentions his four-year tenure as CEO before stepping down. The segment concludes with Barton, gesturing expressively, reflecting on Shazam's early struggles, stating it was "running through a long period of barely surviving," and was "a story of nearly failing, nearly going bankrupt, incredible resilience."

The video opens with Chris Barton, Co-founder of Shazam, speaking directly to the camera in a modern office with a cityscape visible behind him. Wearing a light blue button-up shirt, he explains in a clear, articulate male voice that Shazam 'didn't really hit a hockey stick of incredible user adoption until the App Store launched in 2008.' A female narrator then states, 'Eventually, Apple bought Shazam in 2018,' as the scene transitions to a smartphone displaying the Shazam app interface with an overlaid Apple logo, followed by a Wall Street Journal title card announcing 'Apple Acquires Shazam and Its Song-Recognition App' dated December 11, 2017. The narrator continues, highlighting Shazam as 'one of the most popular free music apps in the App Store.' Chris Barton's voice returns, discussing the evolution of music consumption: 'Once you're paying for unlimited streaming of music, what is it you're actually going to listen to? Because you have access to everything as much as you want.' He elaborates that 'Shazam solves one form of discovery,' allowing users to discover music 'in your daily life.' The female narrator then explains that 'The technology behind Shazam has grown significantly since Apple's acquisition in 2018,' giving it access to 'Apple Music's large library,' and that 'Today, Shazam can identify most of the Apple Music catalog of over 100 million songs.' Chris Barton is then seen demonstrating the Shazam app on his phone, holding it up as it displays 'Listening for music,' then recognized songs like 'Después de la Playa' by Bad Bunny and 'Wild Child' by The Black Keys with Apple Music buttons, and a history screen showing 'Riptide' and 'Free.' He remarks, 'We created an app many years before apps actually existed. Part of the art of a startup is the timing aspect, and so fortunately we found a way to survive through those tough years.' The video then shows the Shazam icon integrated into Apple’s Control Center, with Chris Barton smiling and nodding. The segment concludes with black credit screens listing various contributors and sources, accompanied by light, upbeat instrumental background music.
